that being said let's kick it off and explore some of the mistakes you can avoid when visiting Vienna for the first time number one forgetting about cats when traveling to the heart of Europe many tourists assume that they're visiting a modern and cash-free city but once arrived at the Vienna International Airport where they need to Insert Coins to get a luggage trolley tourists quickly realized that they should carry some cash many austrians still prefer cash over card payments one of the main reasons are the high transaction costs of card payments which results in many vendors rejecting cards for smaller purchases even if a restaurant accepts card payments for your meal most of the time you're not able to tip the waiter by card so when you're traveling in Austria make sure you carry cash with you it's even more extreme the outskirts of Vienna in some places you might have to take a long walk just to find the next ATM talking about long walks brings us to our mistake number two wrong shoes Vienna's most famous streets are not made for high heels or slippery flip-flops Vienna City Center is full of paving stones which makes it really uncomfortable to walk on if you don't wear appropriate Footwear during the cold months enjoying a walk on some of these streets can quickly turn into a bad slippery experience if you don't wear the right shoes exploring Vienna City Center by foot is a must do which makes it even more important to wear comfortable shoes to be able to cover longer walking distances and enjoy the beautiful architecture in the city Vibes however make sure you select the right date to enjoy Vienna City Center which brings us to number three Sundays if you're traveling from a metropolitan City to Vienna you might be surprised how serious austrians take their Sundays almost all shops and many restaurants are closed on Sundays which turns the normal vibrant crowded City Center into an abandoned city if you're planning to schedule your shopping day for a Sunday be assured that it will be a very cheap shopping Sunday s in Austria are mostly dedicated to outdoor activities and catching up with friends and families however sightseeing spots like museums and castles are normally open on Sundays so make sure you check their opening before planning your visit when planning your sightseeing activities one spot needs special attention therefore check out our number four south tower of the Saint Stephen's Cathedral when visiting the sun sea Saint Stephen's Cathedral many tourists feel obliged to climb up the towers which is legit but be aware that there are two ways to get to the Top If you don't do your research take a 50 50 chance you might be climbing up the South Tower of the cathedral which is only accessible via very tight staircase and around 350 stairs once arrived at the top you will be surprised how limited the view is you have to share some small windows with many other tourists to enjoy the beautiful Skyline of Vienna if you like the experience of walking up a tight staircase it's definitely an experience we don't want you to miss but we would recommend you to visit the North Tower instead there is an elevator and you can get great panoramic view of the Vienna Skyline and it's also a great spot to appreciate the details which went into building the Saint Stephen's Cathedral however if you still prefer walking up the stairs make sure to stay hydrated which brings us to our number five buying water many tourists visiting Vienna don't think about it and end up buying bottles of water throughout their whole trip but here's a money saver Vienna has one of the best tap Waters in the world you can drink from every tap and it's going to be refreshing and also healthy if you forgot your water bottle buy a high quality water bottle once and then fill it up as you go during warmer months you will find tap water almost everywhere in public places during winter months it's slightly more challenging but still plenty of possibilities to fill up your water bottle so save some money and your environment by not buying bottled water in Vienna another money saving and environmental friendly advice is our Point number six renting a car in Vienna Vienna has one of the best public transportation systems in the world and it's super affordable for around 17 Euros you get a weekly card which gives you unlimited access to Vienna's public transportation system and operates 24 hours on weekends [Music] thank you during the week you can rely on it from 5am till midnight Vienna has 23 videos.transcript: In this video, I'll be sharing an honest take on the best things to do in Vienna. From palaces and pastries to unique hidden gems, so be sure to stick around until the end for a free map of all these spots and some lesser-known must-dos. Now in case you're new here, hi, I'm Christina from HappyToWander.com and this channel is all about practical travel advice from someone who's been there, done that, so be sure to like and subscribe if that sounds like your kind of thing. Now, let's get started in the very center of the city with a big one and I do mean that literally! Known as one of the largest palace complexes in the world, our first Vienna must-do is to explore the Hofburg. Originally built in the Middle Ages as the seat of the Dukes of Austria, this sprawling palace complex is still used today by the Austrian Federal President as an official residence and workplace. But the Hofburg is probably best known as the OG center of the Habsburg court, where endless meetings, balls, feasts, and more were held for centuries. Today, the Hofburg houses a truly astounding number of Vienna's top attractions. As a quick tip for saving money, these attractions do add up quickly, so it might be worth getting something like the Vienna Pass if you plan to do many of them, as most of them are included. Though if you're short on time or funds, it's still worth at least admiring this beautiful complex from the outside. Some of the most famous highlights here include the Sisi Museum, which is dedicated to the life of the famous Austro-Hungarian Empress Elizabeth, known as Sisi, and houses a number of beautiful personal items including some of her most iconic portraits and dresses. There's also the Vienna State Spanish Riding School here, though the morning training isn't really worth the money or wait, in my opinion. You can also find the Imperial Treasury, which is one of the most important treasure chambers in the world, with two imperial crowns on display along with many other incredible pieces. Plus plenty of green spaces like Burggarten and Volksgarten, along with Heldenplatz, which has its epic gate, and actually more museums if you can believe it in the Neue Burg, but let's address those separately as our next must-do, which is to enjoy the museums of the Neue Burg. This especially noteworthy section of the Hofburg is so named for its relatively speaking recent addition beginning in 1891, the last expansion made to the complex. Today, this photogenic extension is home to a number of museums, including the House of Habsburg Tour which I honestly think is one of the most worthwhile museum experiences in Vienna, especially if you want to learn more about the city's imperial history. This immersive experience guides you through various rooms in the museum, teaching you about Habsburg history as you go. Really worth it and you get to see some very badass armor and treasures. The World Museum is also housed in the same building, though to be honest, I don't think this would be my top priority when visiting Vienna. In this museum you'll find the collection of seafarer James Cook and other treasures from around the world. Out of the many possible cultural attractions to enjoy Vienna, I feel like this one speaks the least to Austrian history and culture, so I would probably prioritize other sites like the House of Austrian History, for instance, which is also housed in this building. This small museum gives a broader overview of Austria's history beyond the Hapsburgs. If you're curious to see Austria's historic timeline laid out in a clear and straightforward way, with relics and helpful explanations for each era, this is a great educational spot. And finally, one of the more underrated though small museums in the complex is the Ephesos Museum, home to artifacts from the Turkish city of Ephesus. I loved having this space entirely to myself during my visit, so definitely head here if you're looking for a bit more of a hidden secret. You'll also find the Austrian National Library here, which is home to the Papyrus Museum, where you'll find an extensive collection of ancient manuscripts, letters, and legal texts. Do not get confused though, a lot of visitors come to this Austrian National Library location in search of the stunning Prunksaal, or State Hall of the Austrian National Library, which is definitely worth seeing but is actually located elsewhere in the complex over in Josefsplatz. But I do think it deserves its own entry, so another beautiful must-do in Vienna is to enjoy the amazing Prunksaal. This opulent Baroque hall houses a wealth of historical books and manuscripts, with some dating all the way back to 1501. Truly, this is one of the most beautiful library rooms in the world, with giant bookcases, marble columns, incredible statues, a pair of giant globes, and some absolutely incredible ceiling frescoes. There are more than 200,000 historic books held here, mostly behind glass to protect them but it's definitely a must-see for any book nerd. Now moving on, of course, no visit to Vienna is complete without a quick gawk at St. Stephen's Cathedral, a Gothic masterpiece that has graced the city since the 12th century. Famed for its multicolored tiled roof and four towers, today visitors can climb up for panoramic views or, perhaps spookier, explore the catacombs below, which house the tombs of notable figures from the city's past. Speaking of tombs, a short walk away from the iconic Stephansdom, you'll find one of the spookier things to do in Vienna, which is a visit to the Capuchin's Crypt, which has served as the burial place of Habsburg dynasty members since 1618. Yes, it's exactly as it sounds, a crypt holding the remains of Habsburgs spanning centuries, including big names like Maria Terresa and, of course, Empress Elizabeth. Sure, it's a bit of a morbid attraction but one that's nonetheless quite interesting if you're into the history of the Habsburg dynasty. Though they vary widely in detail and opulence, many of the sarcophagi are accompanied by informative signs about the often short lives of those who occupy them, providing some eye-opening insight into just how easy it was to die randomly back in the day. But if you're looking for one of the best free activities to enjoy in Vienna, I can definitely suggest a leisurely stroll around Vienna's Ring Road; a grand 5.3 kilometer boulevard circling the city center, filled with some of the city's most drool-worthy architectural wonders. Along the way you'll encounter stunning landmarks like the Vienna State Opera House, which was the first major building to even be constructed along this road. Best enjoyed through a performance here, if you can catch one, or you can also book a guided tour. You'll also find the Austrian Parliament building, which is a Greek Revival style building known for its pretty fountain up front; the City Hall, which is housed in an impressive neo-Gothic building and is, of course, where the mayor of Vienna works along with the city council. It is beautiful to photograph from the outside but you can also join a guided tour for free on select weekdays, so be sure to look into that. And of course, there's the Palace of Justice, a lesser visited gem that's beautiful and free to admire on the inside, with a nice rooftop restaurant as well offering sublime views. I personally think the best way to take in all these views is by foot, but many of the tram lines will also allow you to DIY your own sightseeing tour. Close by you'll find another treasure of a museum: the Albertina, worth visiting for the stunning view from its terrace alone and also a great place to munch on a sausage from the famous Bitzinger Sausage Stand below. Honestly speaking though, if it's value for money you want, the Albertina is probably one of the best all-around attractions in Vienna. Not only is it housed in a stunning Habsburg residential palace, inside you'll also find a vast collection of artwork scattered across four floors with some of the most famous names in art history, ranging from Monet to Picasso. Honestly, I couldn't believe the number of famous names casually on display here! Not only that though, there are also staterooms included in your visit, with 20 restored Habsburg staterooms where you can feel like you've traveled back in time, surrounded by chandeliers, stunning furniture and more art. So yes, I'm a huge fan of the Albertina and can definitely recommend it if you're unsure about which museums to check off your list. Speaking of worthwhile museums, of all the very grand buildings to visit in Vienna, one of my personal favorites is the stunning Kunsthistorisches Museum. which is home to an exceptional collection of European masterpieces. Inside, you'll find the largest art museum in the country, filled with artworks that were once part of the Hapsburg's private collection. Today, highlights include paintings by big names like Raphael, Rembrandt, and Vermeer, along with Egyptian, Greek, and Roman antiquities, sculptures, coins, and books. Best of all though, these works are housed in a jaw-dropping Renaissance Revival building with a stunning café in the middle that is truly a work of art entirely on its own. While it gets busy here there's definitely a good reason for that and if you're an art lover, you won't be disappointed. Across the way, there's also the Natural History Museum, housed in another impressive building because Vienna, of course, likes to show off. That's not all, of course. A short walk away you'll also encounter one of the largest cultural hubs in the world; the aptly named MuseumsQuartier. Established in 2001, this area was once home to the Habsburg Imperial Stables, originally dating back to the 18th century. Today it's a vibrant cultural hub with highlights including museums dedicated to architecture, modern art, contemporary and international art, Austrian art, and even a kid's museum. So needless to say, there is plenty to do! Alright, moving outside of the historic city center, now let's go westwards and discuss one of Vienna's most famous must-sees, which is Schönbrunn Palace. Home to a shocking 1,441 staterooms, Schönbrunn Palace is an epic sprawling palace considered one of the top sites in Vienna, with UNESCO World Heritage status to flaunt along with a wide variety of on-site attractions, with highlights including the world's oldest zoo still in operation, the Imperial Carriage Museum - which I genuinely enjoyed, with over 161 beautifully preserved and marked carriages on display, including some very historically significant ones especially pertaining to Empress Sisi - as well as the state apartments and many, many, more sights. While I do think the palace and its grounds are a must-see, I must confess my disappointment with some of the pricier paid attractions. The Standard State Apartments tour, for instance, is so brief I actually laughed out loud when I realized it was over. So if you want to see more of the palace, you should definitely book one of the longer tours. There's also a virtual reality experience that gives you a quick rundown of the Habsburg Dynasty, which is all right but I probably wouldn't pay full price for it and neither would most people because I was genuinely the only person in that entire room. Though I admit it was nice to sit down for 20 minutes. Finally, other on-site attractions include the Palm Garden and Desert House, both nice enough but not especially unique to Vienna. The Desert House did have some delightful giant tortoises though. There's also a children's museum that is very much geared towards kids with play and dress-up stations, so not particularly worth doing unless you're traveling with kids in tow. Now, you might be wondering, are all of these things a must-do? Not if you're short on time, in my honest opinion. But if you have the Vienna Pass and want to take some time to adequately explore the palace and all its sights, then spending a full day here would be a great bet. Another one of Vienna's main regal attractions can be found at Belvedere Palace, originally built in the late 17th century as a summer residence for Prince Eugene of Savoy. Today there are two palaces here to explore: Upper Belvedere, known for its Baroque design and art collection including The Kiss by Gustav Klimt; and the Lower Belvedere, which houses temporary exhibitions of art and cultural history. On site you'll also find the Belvedere 21 Museum of Contemporary Art, originally built as a pavilion for the 1958 World Exhibition, with a focus on modern and contemporary art, and there's also a pretty Baroque garden as well. Just northeast of Belvedere in the district of Langstrasse though, you'll find a number of other interesting attractions and another Vienna must-do, in my opinion, which is to admire the Hundertwasser side of the city. In a city as elegant as Vienna, several buildings designed by renowned architect Friedensreich Hundertwasser add a splash of color and whimsy, and seeing them - I think - is a must for any architecture lover. Now an Instagram hotspot, one prominent Hundertwasser location is the Hundertwasser House, completed in 1985 as an innovative public housing project. Do note that this house is mainly just a photo op but the café right next to the house has a nice terrace where you can admire the photogenic façade with a hot coffee and warm apple strudel. The Hundertwasser Village across the street is also nice to see on the inside, though beware that most of the shops kind of just sell generic souvenirs. I will say though, if you're interested in his work, I can definitely recommend visiting the KunstHausWien, less than a 10-minute walk away. This is where you can truly appreciate Hundertwasser's work over the years and also explore the inside of one of his most beautiful creations. Housed in a former furniture factory, just like the Hundertwasser House, this museum features irregular shapes, colorful mosaics, and plenty of greenery, as well as the only permanent collection of his work. Oh, and he also designed a stunning incinerator out in the ninth district, if you're interested. Speaking of districts, another Vienna must-do for those with the time is venturing out of the very central first district, which is where most tourists tend to spend all their time. Yes, it's beautiful and historic but there's a lot more to see in the city, all around its 23 different districts, many of which have a distinctive vibe and personality. Out in the second district, for instance, you can visit the famous Vienna Prater. Granted, I wouldn't go out of my way to come here but if you're especially enthusiastic about amusement parks, then this amusement park is definitely worth a stop. Dating back to 1895, the Vienna Prater is an amusement park located on what was once the hunting grounds of Austrian royalty. A key highlight here is the giant Ferris wheel, the Riesenrad, as seen in Before Sunrise, which is cute and offers some great panoramic views of the city. Do beware though that it moves super slowly, so it isn't really the most exciting of rides. Madame Tussauds can also be found here but honestly, I wouldn't prioritize it unless it's included with a pass, and there's also the peculiar Republic of Kugelmugel, an 8 square meter spherical object that was once declared its own republic and micronation. While on this side of the river, another potential viewpoint to check off your list out in the 22nd district is the soaring Danube Tower, which stands at 252 meters high, offering sweeping views over the city. For me personally, I prefer closer-up views in the historic center where you can better appreciate the centuries-old buildings, but if you prefer sweeping far-away panoramas, this might be one to consider. Now there's a lot of charm and personality to Vienna's different districts, but the draw of many is that they have nice coffee spots, restaurants, and places to hang out and shop. So if you're looking for a slower paced trip, exploring out of the center is perfect. If however, you do want to just get a bit more off the beaten path, while still doing somewhat touristy activities, the 19th is probably a good bet. This fancy district is home to vineyards, villas, and heuriger: wine taverns that serve small bites made in-house along with house-made wine, a great relaxing addition to a busy itinerary. Speaking of relaxing additions, another one of the more relaxing things to do in Vienna is to enjoy the city's café culture. Vienna is full of grand cafés thanks to its history after the second Turkish siege in 1683. According to legend, soldiers of the Polish Hapsburg army found some coffee beans that had been left behind by the Turks and one of the officers used those beans to open the first coffee house. These coffee houses then became popular gathering places for intellectuals, artists, and writers, and the legacy has stuck. Today, Viennese coffee house culture is listed as an intangible cultural heritage of Austria by UNESCO because it's just that iconic. So in between museum jaunts, one of the best things to do is grab a coffee and a sweet treat, ideally in a grand majestic setting. There's of course, plenty of options, whether you're looking for historic and grand coffee houses like Café Central or Café Sacher, where the prices and crowds will definitely remind you that they're popular with tourists, but if your primary objective is coffee, there are plenty more modern options. Though no matter where you go, do note that they kind of have their own coffee language here, so be sure to read up on different drink types and names before your trip, or consult the written version of this guide for more info. I also have a full Vienna travel tips video as well that outlines the basics. As a warning though, Viennese coffee has a reputation for being unexpectedly strong, anecdotally causing many tourists to sprint to the bathroom afterwards. So yeah, just be careful! On a related note, you really can't leave Vienna without having your fill of local treats and eats. For efficiency's sake there are definitely tons of food tours and even cooking classes you can book, but some absolute must-tries for your own DIY tour include Wiener Schnitzel, a traditional Austrian dish consisting of a breaded and fried veal cutlet often served with lemon, potato salad or lingonberry jam; Käsekrainer, which is an Austrian sausage filled with melted cheese, typically grilled or fried and often served with mustard and bread; and just honestly, pastries in general, which I know is so broad but Vienna is famous for them and there's way too many to list. Some classics include apfelstrudel, which is pastry filled with apple and raisins; topfenstrudel, which is pastry filled with a creamy curd cheese; and kipferl, a crescent-shaped pastry often filled with nuts or jam. Finally, this is just your reminder to go beyond the tourist trail and find your own hidden gems. Vienna is a huge city with so much to do beyond just the main tourist highlights mentioned in this video, so if you have the time, be sure to look into more offbeat activities too that suit your niche interests. For instance, Vienna is full of super-small niche museums, whether you're into clocks, criminals, coffee, contraception, pathological anatomy or globes! As a travel blogger, I of course, had to check out the Globe Museum, which had a fairly impressive collection including globes of other planets, which weirdly I never considered was a thing. So yes, I encourage you to find your own small museums to nerd out about because there's really something for everyone. And if you're interested in churches, Vienna has many more to offer beyond the famous Stephansdom. Karlskirche is one of my personal favorites and a common thing to do here is attend a classical music concert, although there are plenty more options to check out as well, so be sure to indulge your curiosity and stop by a few during your trip. Finally, we've spoken a lot about Vienna's elegant imperial architecture but there are plenty of intriguing modern structures scattered around the city as well. Some highlights include the Modernist Secession Building, home to exhibitions of contemporary art; Wotruba Church, which is a bold brutalist building made of massive concrete blocks; DC Towers, aka Austria's tallest skyscraper, and many more. All to say, there's plenty more to do in Vienna so exploring local blogs is always a good idea to get a feel for what locals might like to do for fun. Visiting vienna.com, for instance, is a great resource. Alright, be sure to check out this link for a full map that lists all the locations from this video, and be sure to like and subscribe for more travel videos just like this one. I'll see you next time, bye!

videos.transcript: All right, picture this. You've just landed in Vienna, ready to be swept away by imperial palaces, charming cafes, and a little bit of Mozart magic. H you step out and are excited to dive into everything this city has to offer. But within minutes, you realize, uh-oh, nobody's coming to take your order at the cafe. You're getting sideeyed for standing on the wrong side of the escalator. And wait, why is the shop closed on a Sunday? If any of this sounds familiar or something you really want to avoid, don't worry, we've got you. If you're new around here, we are Katy and Pura living in Vienna for almost all our lives. In this channel, we share our insights on this beautiful city that we get to call home. Vienna is a city full of charm, history, and hidden rules that aren't always obvious to visitors. But if you know these insider tips, you'll feel like a local in no time. In 2024, Vienna had around 6.4 million visitors from abroad. That's a plus of 11.3% compared to 2023 according to the tourism board. So, the city is becoming more popular. And today we're spilling the tea on seven common mistakes first timers make in Vienna in 2025 and of course how to avoid them like a pro. Let's jump right into it. Mistake number one, not understanding vianese restaurant etiquette. Imagine this. You're at Cafe Santal, one of Fienna's most famous coffee houses. You take a seat excited for that Vina Milange and a slice of their actually pretty amazing up for stood. But nobody comes to take your order. 5 minutes pass, then 10, you start wondering, did they forget about me? No, this is just classic vianese cafe culture. Unlike other places where waiters immediately rush to your table, in Vienna's traditional coffee houses, service is a bit more relaxed, or as some might say, slow. This isn't bad service. It's part of the experience. People come here to linger, not to rush. This tradition dates back to the 17th century when coffee houses became social hubs where intellectuals and artists debated for hours. So, how to avoid this mistake? If you're in a hurry, look for places with sedum, which is self-service, which is common in bakeries and fast casual spots. In traditional coffee houses, make eye contact with the waiter or give them a slight nod when you're ready to order. Then they'll get the hint and come to your table. Mistake number two, not tipping or tipping incorrectly. In Vienna, tipping is not just nice, it's expected. In many countries, tipping is a nice bonus, but in Austria, waiters rely on it, or at least it's expected. That said, tipping should always be voluntary and a reflection of the service you received. You should never feel pressured to tip, but understanding the local etiquette can help you avoid awkward moments. We actually created a dedicated video explaining the tipping culture in Vienna, breaking down where this expectation comes from and how much is appropriate. Be sure to check it out before you visit. Just scan the QR code or click the link in the top right corner. But for now, here's a quick summary based on our 30 plus years of experience living in Vienna. How much should you tip? Well, a 10% tip is standard for good service. For example, if your bill is €18, round it up to €20. If the service was average, rounding up to the nearest euro is perfectly fine. Even if your bill states service included, tipping a couple of euros is still appreciated. Pro tip, in Vienna, don't leave your tip on the table. Instead, tell the waiter the total amount you'd like to pay before they process the bill. This is the local way of handling tips and ensures the correct amount is charged right away. Mistake number three, ignoring queuing etiquette. Vienna is a wellorganized city, but when it comes to queuing, let's just say it's different. In small bakeries or even some supermarkets, there is no clear line to order your sandwich or bread. There's often no visible cue, just an unspoken rule where everyone keeps track of who arrived first. You cut a line and you might earn yourself an icy glare or a sharp dea for which translates to the gentleman was ahead of you. Public transport is another game entirely. At tram and bus stops, don't expect British style cues. Instead, there's a simple rule. Let passengers exit before boarding. You fail to follow this and you might get a firm which translates to you have to let people off first. During rush hour, things can get intense. After all, with 4,414 tra and bus stops citywide, efficiency is key. How to avoid this awkwardness? Well, at least at bakeries or supermarket, take a second look around and mentally note who was there before you. 15% of tourists accidentally skip this and unknowingly cut in. At trans and bus stops, step aside and let people off first. Mistake number four, littering or not using the bin. Vienna is one of the cleanest cities in Europe, and locals take pride in keeping it that way. In fact, 83% of residents gave the city's cleanliness a thumbs up in a 2023 survey. With Vienna aiming for net zero waste by 2040, it's no surprise that littering isn't taken lightly. Vienna's bins actually come with their own personality. You will definitely spot cheeky messages like keep Vienna clean. Even your grandma would approve. These messages and the marketing efforts are part of MA48's plan to engage residents and visitors reinforcing the city's clean culture. Such initiatives are common in public spaces like parks, street and public transport stations to promote responsible waste disposal. Mistake number five, assuming that you can pay everywhere with card even in 2025. Card usage in Vienna is robust, driven by the city's modernity and tourist influx. Mastercard's 2022 report on contactless payments states that 85% of Austrian merchants accept contactless payments with expectations of growth. However, based on a study done by the Austrian National Bank from 2023, cash is still the most important payment method in Austria. 63% of all transactions are still cash transactions in 2023. The number is decreasing, but it's still the most popular compared to 30% for card payments in 2023. While card usage is increasing, the shift is pretty slow. So, don't be surprised if some businesses still require cash. Pro tip, always carry some coins. You'll need them for supermarket trolleys or public restrooms. If you're low on change, ask your hotel receptionist. They're usually happy to help. Mistake number six, eating on public transport. So, picture this. You just grabbed your favorite snack from a vianese sausage stand. You hop on to the uban, unwrap it, and take a bite only to notice a dozen disapproving stairs. But why? Because eating on public transport is strictly banned in Vienna. This rule has been in place since 2019, and Vinolinian's 2024 report shows an impressive 95% compliance rate. While there's no immediate alarm if you sneak a quiet bite, but don't be surprised if you get some judgmental looks or even a €50 fine if caught by an inspector. It's not just about keeping trains clean. Austrians take public etiquette very seriously. Pro tip: If you're hungry and you grab yourself some takeaway food, finish your food before hopping onto the subway. or even better, if the weather allows, save it for a park bench instead and enjoy the beautiful city. Speaking of food, Purya and I are actually real foodies and we recently launched our own downloadable maps that you guys can purchase on our website. We have started off with our restaurant guide. You will get a Google Maps link with all of our favorite food spots in Vienna. Plus, we'll keep it updated regularly so you'll never miss out on any of the new hotspots. Link is in the description box below. Now, moving on to mistake number seven, standing on the wrong side of the escalator. In Vienna, the rule is simple. Stand on the right side of the escalators and keep the left side free for those walking up the escalator. This is a standard in many European cities and helps keep traffic moving, especially at busy subway stations like Stefan Plats or Kplats. It's not just about speed, it's about curtsy, and locals take it pretty seriously. The rule here is simple. Stand right, walk left. Vienna is an incredible city full of history, culture, and charm. But like any destination, it has its quirks. From understanding local restaurant etiquette to navigating public transport rules, a little local know-how will go a long way in making your visit smoother and more enjoyable. Avoiding these common first hanger mistakes won't just help you blend in like a pro, but it'll also earn you a few appreciative nods from the locals. So, as you explore Vienna's grand palaces, cozy cafes, and vibrant markets, keep these tips in mind. Respect the city's traditions, follow the unwritten rules, and you'll have an unforgettable experience in one of Europe's most beautiful capitals. Now, go enjoy that Vina Milange and Quizaka, just not on the Uban. Thank you guys for watching. If you like this video, please give it a thumbs up and subscribe to our channel. And we'll see you guys in the next video. Bye.
